title: Dog Nose Bleeds (Epistaxis): Causes and Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Spontaneous nose bleeds in dogs are a high-urgency concern. Learn about epistaxis, when to seek emergency care, and how to help your vet triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Spontaneous nasal bleeding in dogs is a high-urgency symptom that requires immediate veterinary attention if it persists for more than five minutes or is accompanied by lethargy. Providing a photo or video of the bleeding helps veterinarians quickly assess the severity and prepare for treatment.

What is spontaneous nasal bleeding in dogs and what causes it?

Spontaneous bleeding from the nose, clinically known as epistaxis, is the sudden discharge of blood from one or both nostrils without an obvious external injury. This condition is a symptom of an underlying medical issue that could range from:

  • Localized nasal infections
  • Serious systemic clotting disorders
  • Nasal tumors
  • Rodenticide (rat poison) ingestion

Is it an emergency if my dog’s nose starts bleeding spontaneously?

The urgency level for spontaneous nasal bleeding is High. You must seek immediate emergency veterinary care if:

  • The bleeding is profuse or does not stop within five minutes.
  • The bleeding is accompanied by pale gums or lethargy.
  • The bleeding is the result of life-threatening conditions such as toxin ingestion or severe clotting deficiencies.

How does taking a photo or video of my dog’s nosebleed help with triage?

Taking a clear photo or short video while the bleeding is occurring is incredibly helpful for your veterinarian. A visual record allows the medical team to:

  • Assess the volume of blood loss.
  • Determine if the bleeding is unilateral (one nostril) or bilateral (both nostrils).
  • Identify the presence of clots.
  • Prioritize your dog’s case and prepare the necessary diagnostic tools before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, epistaxis, or nosebleeds, can be caused by oronasal neoplasia, dental disease resulting in oronasal fistula, nasal foreign bodies, or nasopharyngeal disease such as foreign bodies or mass lesions. Diagnostic procedures should include a complete blood count, serum chemistry profile, and urinalysis to rule out concurrent systemic disease. If epistaxis is present, coagulation testing, including prothrombin time, partial thromboplastin time, and buccal mucosal bleeding time, should be considered in addition to a platelet count. Imaging of the nasal chambers via nasal/dental radiographs or advanced imaging modalities like CT is essential. Rhinoscopy, retroflex nasopharyngoscopy, nasal flushing, and nasal biopsy are useful diagnostic tools but should be delayed until after imaging to avoid iatrogenic changes that may obscure the primary disease.

Chapter: Otorhinolaryngology, Diagnostic Imaging, Clinical Pathology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1480)

faqs.question: What should I do immediately if my dog's nose starts bleeding?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog as calm as possible to prevent their blood pressure from rising. You may gently apply a cold compress to the bridge of the nose, but do not insert anything into the nostrils.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human medication to stop the bleed?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications like aspirin or ibuprofen, as these can interfere with blood clotting and significantly worsen the bleeding.

faqs.question: What are common causes of epistaxis in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include nasal trauma, foreign bodies like foxtails, severe dental infections, fungal infections, rodenticide poisoning, and various types of cancer.
